content.text: BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers, Devlin et al., 2018) produces contextual embeddings the same way ELMo does — a different vector per word occurrence — but uses a Transformer encoder instead of LSTMs. Every token attends to every other token simultaneously, so "bank" in "banking rules" sees "rules" in the same layer pass as it sees "bank", with no directionality constraint.

attrs.alt: BERT input construction: three embedding types (token, segment A/B, position 0–7) are summed for each of two sentences separated by [SEP]. Arrows show the signal passing through 12 Transformer layers. The [CLS] final vector is used for classification; individual token vectors are used for NER/tagging. Right panel shows which layer to extract for different use-cases.

content.text: BERT's input is the sum of three learned embedding tables:
: WordPiece vocabulary of ~30 000 sub-word tokens. Unknown words are split into known pieces: "banking" → ["bank", "##ing"].
: vectors A or B identify which of the two input sentences each token belongs to. Used during next-sentence prediction pretraining.
: learned vectors for positions 0–511. Unlike sinusoidal PE in the original Transformer, these are trained from scratch.
Two special tokens are always present:
at position 0 (its final hidden state becomes the sentence-level representation) and
at the end of each sentence segment.

from transformers import BertTokenizer, BertModel
import torch

tokenizer = BertTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model     = BertModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased", output_hidden_states=True)
model.eval()

sentences = [
    "I went to the bank to deposit money.",
    "The fox sat on the river bank.",
]

for sent in sentences:
    inputs = tokenizer(sent, return_tensors="pt")
    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**inputs)

    # outputs.last_hidden_state: (1, seq_len, 768)
    # outputs.hidden_states: tuple of 13 tensors (embedding + 12 layers)
    last_hidden = outputs.last_hidden_state   # shape (1, seq_len, 768)
    all_layers  = outputs.hidden_states       # tuple of 13 × (1, seq_len, 768)

    tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(inputs["input_ids"][0])
    for i, tok in enumerate(tokens):
        print(f"{tok:<12} {last_hidden[0, i, :4].tolist()}")
Polysemy check: same word, different vectors

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from transformers import BertTokenizer, BertModel

tokenizer = BertTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model     = BertModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model.eval()

def get_token_vec(sentence, target_word):
    inputs = tokenizer(sentence, return_tensors="pt")
    tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(inputs["input_ids"][0])
    with torch.no_grad():
        out = model(**inputs)
    idx = next(i for i, t in enumerate(tokens) if t == target_word)
    return out.last_hidden_state[0, idx]

vec_finance = get_token_vec(
    "I went to the bank to deposit money.", "bank"
)
vec_river   = get_token_vec(
    "The fox sat on the river bank.", "bank"
)

cos_sim = F.cosine_similarity(vec_finance.unsqueeze(0),
                               vec_river.unsqueeze(0)).item()
print(f"Cosine similarity: {cos_sim:.3f}")
# ~0.72 — same word type, different contextual embeddings
# (Word2Vec would give 1.0 — identical vectors)
BERT has 12 layers (base) or 24 layers (large), each producing a 768-dim (or 1024-dim) hidden state per token. Different layers capture different information:

# Sum of last 4 hidden layers (common NER recipe)
import torch
from transformers import BertTokenizer, BertModel

tokenizer = BertTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model     = BertModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased", output_hidden_states=True)
model.eval()

text   = "I went to the bank to deposit money."
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt")

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs)

hidden_states = outputs.hidden_states  # tuple: 13 × (1, T, 768)

# Stack last 4 layers and sum
token_vecs = torch.stack(hidden_states[-4:]).sum(dim=0)  # (1, T, 768)
print(token_vecs.shape)   # torch.Size([1, 9, 768])

vector is not a good sentence embedding for semantic similarity — it was never trained for that directly. Mean-pooling all token vectors performs better. For production sentence embeddings, use a model fine-tuned with contrastive loss (e.g. Sentence-BERT):

# pip install sentence-transformers
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

# all-MiniLM-L6-v2 is fast (80 MB) and strong on semantic similarity
model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

sentences = [
    "I went to the bank to deposit money.",
    "She made a withdrawal at the ATM.",
    "The fox sat on the river bank.",
]

embeddings = model.encode(sentences)   # shape (3, 384)

from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
sim = cosine_similarity(embeddings)
print(sim.round(2))
# [[1.   0.82 0.31]
#  [0.82 1.   0.28]
#  [0.31 0.28 1.  ]]
# Financial sentences are closer together than financial vs. river

BERT-base is usually the right starting point: it fits on a single GPU with a batch size of 32 and reaches within ~1 % of BERT-large on most tasks after fine-tuning. Use BERT-large when the dataset is small and you need maximum accuracy from frozen features.

Before contextual embeddings, NLP pipelines relied on static word vectors (Word2Vec, GloVe, fastText) that assigned a single fixed vector to each word type regardless of context. The word 'bank' had one vector whether it appeared in 'river bank' or 'bank account'. These static representations forced downstream models to learn disambiguation from context themselves, requiring large amounts of task-specific training data. BERT reversed this: disambiguation happens inside the encoder, and the resulting vectors are already context-sensitive when they arrive at the downstream model.

The practical consequences are substantial. Before BERT, the state of the art on question answering (SQuAD) was about 87% F1; BERT pushed it to 93.2% in a single publication. Named entity recognition, coreference resolution, semantic role labeling, and natural language inference all saw similar step-change improvements. The reason is simple: BERT's 12 or 24 Transformer layers process the entire sentence bidirectionally, allowing each token to attend to every other token in the same pass, building rich representations that capture syntax and semantics simultaneously.

The same token gets a different vector in different sentences. 'Play' in 'play the piano' has a different embedding than 'play' in 'a play at the theatre' — a fundamental improvement over static embeddings.
WordPiece handles out-of-vocabulary words by splitting them into known pieces ('running' → ['run', '##ning']). No word is ever truly unknown.
Unlike GPT-style left-to-right models, BERT sees the entire sentence at once. The token 'bank' in position 3 can attend to 'river' in position 7 in the same layer.
A single BERT-base model fine-tuned for a few epochs achieves competitive results across dozens of NLP tasks, dramatically reducing the data and compute required for each.
Different layers encode different linguistic information (syntax in early layers, semantics in later layers), enabling task-specific feature extraction by selecting the right layer.
BERT models are available in Hugging Face, TensorFlow Hub, and ONNX formats, with hardware-optimised kernels for CPU, GPU, and specialised accelerators.
BERT-base has 110M parameters and runs a forward pass in ~10ms per sentence on GPU. Processing 1 million sentences takes ~3 hours on a single GPU — impractical without batching or distillation.
BERT-base supports at most 512 tokens. Documents longer than this must be truncated or processed in overlapping chunks.
BERT is optimised for understanding tasks (classification, NER, Q&A). It cannot generate text; for generation, GPT-2/3/4 or T5 are used instead.

Using frozen BERT embeddings as features for a downstream classifier is significantly worse than fine-tuning all layers end-to-end. This means you need task-specific labelled data and training infrastructure.
Not optimal for sentence similarity out of the box.
The raw [CLS] token is not a good sentence embedding for cosine similarity. Fine-tuning with a contrastive objective (as Sentence-BERT does) is required for similarity tasks.
bert-base-uncased is trained on English Wikipedia and BookCorpus only. For other languages, use mBERT or dedicated language-specific models.
Named entity recognition (NER): tag each token with its entity type (PERSON, ORG, GPE). Fine-tune BERT with a linear classifier on top of token embeddings.
Sequence classification: sentiment analysis, intent detection, topic classification. Fine-tune using the [CLS] token.
Extractive question answering: find the span in a passage that answers a question (SQuAD style). Fine-tune with start/end position classifiers.
Semantic textual similarity: with a contrastive fine-tuning objective (Sentence-BERT), BERT embeddings become state-of-the-art for sentence similarity.
Feature extraction: use BERT as a frozen feature extractor when you have limited labelled data, then train a lightweight classifier on top.
Text generation tasks — BERT cannot autoregressively generate tokens.
Real-time classification on CPU with latency < 5ms — use distilled models (DistilBERT, TinyBERT) or static embeddings.
Documents much longer than 512 tokens without chunking — Longformer or BigBird handle long documents natively.
When you have < 500 training examples and cannot fine-tune — few-shot prompting with a large LLM may outperform fine-tuned BERT.
How to Fine-Tune BERT for Classification
Load a pre-trained BERT model and its tokeniser from Hugging Face.
Add a classification head: a linear layer mapping [CLS] embedding (768 dim) to the number of classes.
Tokenise your training data with padding and truncation to 512 tokens.
Fine-tune all layers (or the top N layers) with a small learning rate (2e-5 to 5e-5).
Train for 3–5 epochs with weight decay 0.01 and warmup steps.
Evaluate on a held-out validation set and select the best checkpoint.

from transformers import (
    BertTokenizer, BertForSequenceClassification,
    TrainingArguments, Trainer
)
from datasets import Dataset
import torch
import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, f1_score

# --- 1. Sample training data (replace with your dataset) ---
train_texts  = [
    "I absolutely loved this movie!",
    "The food was terrible and service was slow.",
    "Great value for money, highly recommend.",
    "Disappointing experience, would not return.",
    "Excellent quality, fast delivery.",
    "Broken on arrival, very disappointed.",
]
train_labels = [1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0]   # 1 = positive, 0 = negative

# --- 2. Tokenise ---
tokenizer = BertTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

def tokenise_fn(examples):
    return tokenizer(
        examples["text"],
        truncation=True,
        max_length=128,
        padding="max_length",
    )

ds = Dataset.from_dict({"text": train_texts, "label": train_labels})
ds = ds.map(tokenise_fn, batched=True)
ds = ds.train_test_split(test_size=0.33, seed=42)

# --- 3. Load model with classification head ---
model = BertForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    "bert-base-uncased",
    num_labels=2,
)

# --- 4. Define metrics ---
def compute_metrics(eval_pred):
    logits, labels = eval_pred
    preds = np.argmax(logits, axis=1)
    return {
        "accuracy": accuracy_score(labels, preds),
        "f1":       f1_score(labels, preds, average="weighted"),
    }

# --- 5. Training configuration ---
training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir          = "./bert-sentiment",
    num_train_epochs    = 3,
    per_device_train_batch_size = 8,
    per_device_eval_batch_size  = 8,
    warmup_steps        = 10,
    weight_decay        = 0.01,
    learning_rate       = 2e-5,
    evaluation_strategy = "epoch",
    save_strategy       = "epoch",
    load_best_model_at_end = True,
    logging_steps       = 5,
    seed                = 42,
)

trainer = Trainer(
    model          = model,
    args           = training_args,
    train_dataset  = ds["train"],
    eval_dataset   = ds["test"],
    compute_metrics= compute_metrics,
)

trainer.train()
print("Best checkpoint metrics:", trainer.state.best_metric)

from transformers import pipeline

# Pre-trained NER model (fine-tuned BERT-large)
ner = pipeline(
    "ner",
    model="dbmdz/bert-large-cased-finetuned-conll03-english",
    aggregation_strategy="simple",   # merge sub-word tokens
)

text = "Apple is planning to open a new store in London next to the Thames."
entities = ner(text)

for ent in entities:
    print(f"  [{ent['entity_group']:<5}] '{ent['word']}'  (score={ent['score']:.3f})")
# Output:
#   [ORG  ] 'Apple'  (score=0.999)
#   [LOC  ] 'London'  (score=0.999)
#   [LOC  ] 'Thames'  (score=0.997)

from transformers import pipeline

# BERT fine-tuned on SQuAD 2.0
qa = pipeline(
    "question-answering",
    model="deepset/bert-base-cased-squad2",
)

context = """
The Eiffel Tower was built from 1887 to 1889 by Gustave Eiffel,
whose company specialised in building metal frameworks and structures.
It stands 330 metres tall and was the world's tallest man-made structure
for 41 years, from its completion until the Chrysler Building in New York
was finished in 1930.
"""

questions = [
    "Who built the Eiffel Tower?",
    "How tall is the Eiffel Tower?",
    "When did the Eiffel Tower stop being the tallest structure?",
]

for q in questions:
    result = qa(question=q, context=context)
    print(f"Q: {q}")
    print(f"A: {result['answer']}  (score={result['score']:.3f})")
    print()
Real-World Example: Domain-Specific Fine-Tuning for Legal NER

Law firms and legal-tech companies routinely fine-tune BERT for legal entity recognition: identifying parties, case numbers, statutes, courts, and dates in contracts and court filings. The following example shows the training loop for a legal NER model using token classification.

"""
Legal NER with BERT — illustrates token classification fine-tuning.
Assumes a dataset of (tokens, labels) where labels follow the BIO scheme:
  B-PARTY, I-PARTY, B-COURT, I-COURT, B-DATE, I-DATE, O
"""
from transformers import (
    BertTokenizerFast, BertForTokenClassification,
    DataCollatorForTokenClassification, TrainingArguments, Trainer,
)
from datasets import Dataset
import numpy as np
import evaluate

# BIO label set for legal documents
label2id = {
    "O": 0, "B-PARTY": 1, "I-PARTY": 2,
    "B-COURT": 3, "I-COURT": 4,
    "B-DATE": 5, "I-DATE": 6,
}
id2label = {v: k for k, v in label2id.items()}

# Minimal training example (production would have 10K+ annotated sentences)
train_data = {
    "tokens": [
        ["Apple", "Inc", ".", "v", ".", "Samsung", "Electronics", "Co", "."],
        ["The", "Ninth", "Circuit", "Court", "reversed", "the", "decision", "."],
    ],
    "ner_tags": [
        [1, 2, 0, 0, 0, 1, 2, 2, 0],          # Apple Inc = PARTY, Samsung = PARTY
        [0, 3, 4, 4, 0, 0, 0, 0],              # Ninth Circuit Court = COURT
    ],
}

tokenizer = BertTokenizerFast.from_pretrained("bert-base-cased")

def tokenise_and_align(examples):
    tokenised = tokenizer(
        examples["tokens"],
        is_split_into_words=True,    # already pre-tokenised
        truncation=True,
        max_length=128,
    )
    aligned_labels = []
    for i, labels in enumerate(examples["ner_tags"]):
        word_ids = tokenised.word_ids(batch_index=i)
        new_labels = []
        prev_word  = None
        for word_id in word_ids:
            if word_id is None:
                new_labels.append(-100)     # special token: ignore in loss
            elif word_id != prev_word:
                new_labels.append(labels[word_id])
            else:
                # Sub-word continuation: use I- tag (or -100 to ignore)
                tag = labels[word_id]
                new_labels.append(tag if tag == 0 else tag + 1 if tag % 2 == 1 else tag)
            prev_word = word_id
        aligned_labels.append(new_labels)
    tokenised["labels"] = aligned_labels
    return tokenised

ds = Dataset.from_dict(train_data)
tokenised_ds = ds.map(tokenise_and_align, batched=True)

model = BertForTokenClassification.from_pretrained(
    "bert-base-cased",
    num_labels=len(label2id),
    id2label=id2label,
    label2id=label2id,
)

seqeval = evaluate.load("seqeval")

def compute_metrics(p):
    preds, labels = p
    preds = np.argmax(preds, axis=2)
    true_preds  = [[id2label[p] for (p,l) in zip(pr, la) if l != -100] for pr, la in zip(preds, labels)]
    true_labels = [[id2label[l] for (p,l) in zip(pr, la) if l != -100] for pr, la in zip(preds, labels)]
    return seqeval.compute(predictions=true_preds, references=true_labels)

args = TrainingArguments("legal-bert-ner", num_train_epochs=5, per_device_train_batch_size=2)
collator = DataCollatorForTokenClassification(tokenizer)
trainer  = Trainer(
    model=model, args=args,
    train_dataset=tokenised_ds,
    data_collator=collator,
    compute_metrics=compute_metrics,
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
)
print("Training setup complete. In production, call trainer.train().")
Names, locations, and organisations are case-sensitive. Always use bert-base-cased (or a cased variant) for NER and entity-sensitive tasks.
Ignoring the -100 label for sub-word tokens.

When aligning NER labels to WordPiece sub-word tokens, sub-word continuations must be assigned -100 (ignored in loss) or the model trains on inconsistent supervision. Forgetting this is the single most common bug in BERT fine-tuning for token classification.
Fine-tuning with too high a learning rate.
Learning rates above 5e-5 typically cause catastrophic forgetting of pre-trained weights. Use 2e-5 to 5e-5 with a linear warmup schedule.
Not using [CLS] for the right task.
The [CLS] token is trained for next-sentence prediction, not semantic similarity. For classification, fine-tune the model; for similarity, use Sentence-BERT or mean-pool all tokens.
Padding tokens have attention_mask=0. Always pass the attention_mask tensor to model forward calls, or padding tokens will receive non-trivial attention weights and corrupt the output.
BERT is large enough to memorise training labels in a few epochs. Always evaluate on a separate held-out set and report metrics from the best validation checkpoint.
BioBERT, FinBERT, LegalBERT, CodeBERT, and SciBERT are BERT models pre-trained on domain text. They typically outperform bert-base by 3–8% on domain tasks without any additional pretraining.
Use gradient checkpointing for large batches.
Pass gradient_checkpointing=True to reduce GPU memory usage during fine-tuning, enabling larger batch sizes that stabilise training.
The lower layers of BERT encode general linguistic knowledge; set their learning rate 10–50% lower than the top layers to preserve useful representations.
Pass fp16=True to TrainingArguments to speed up fine-tuning by 1.5–2× with negligible accuracy loss.
Freeze the bottom layers for small datasets.
If you have < 1000 labelled examples, fine-tuning all 12 layers risks overfitting. Freeze layers 1–8 and only update the top 4.
This automatically reloads the checkpoint with the best validation metric, avoiding the common mistake of training until divergence and then using the final (overfit) checkpoint.

Fine-tuning BERT-base on a classification task with 10K examples takes roughly 5 minutes on a single A100 GPU for 3 epochs. Inference throughput at batch_size=32 is approximately 1000 examples/second on an A100. For CPU deployment, use DistilBERT (66M parameters, 97% of BERT quality, 2× faster) or TinyBERT (14.5M parameters, 93% quality, 9× faster).

The memory footprint of BERT-base during inference is approximately 440 MB for the model weights in float32. For GPU batch inference, the key limit is activation memory: at batch_size=64 and sequence_length=512, BERT-base requires approximately 14 GB of GPU memory — use fp16 to halve this to 7 GB.
The definitive library for loading, fine-tuning, and deploying BERT models. Provides Trainer API, pipeline interface, and 1000+ BERT-based checkpoints.
Export fine-tuned BERT to ONNX format with
and serve with ONNX Runtime for 2–5× faster CPU inference compared to PyTorch.
Optimise BERT for Intel CPUs (OpenVINO) or NVIDIA GPUs (TensorRT). TensorRT-optimised BERT achieves 10× speedup over vanilla PyTorch inference.
Topic modelling library that uses BERT embeddings + UMAP + HDBSCAN to discover topics in large text corpora without requiring labelled data.
package allows dropping BERT into a spaCy pipeline as a component, enabling BERT-backed NER, POS, and dependency parsing with spaCy's familiar interface.

# Fast BERT inference with ONNX Runtime
# pip install optimum[onnxruntime]

from optimum.onnxruntime import ORTModelForSequenceClassification
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
import time

model_name = "distilbert-base-uncased-finetuned-sst-2-english"

# Export to ONNX (one-time step)
model = ORTModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    model_name,
    export=True,    # auto-exports to ONNX
)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)

texts = ["I love this product!", "Terrible quality, very disappointed."] * 100

# ONNX Runtime inference (significantly faster than PyTorch on CPU)
inputs = tokenizer(texts, return_tensors="pt", truncation=True,
                   padding=True, max_length=128)

t0 = time.perf_counter()
outputs = model(**inputs)
elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0

import torch
preds = torch.argmax(outputs.logits, dim=1)
print(f"Classified {len(texts)} sentences in {elapsed*1000:.1f}ms")
print(f"Throughput: {len(texts)/elapsed:.0f} sentences/second")
Same as BERT but generally better; preferred over bert-base

BERT-based classifiers are vulnerable to adversarial text substitutions (e.g., replacing a word with a synonym that changes the model's prediction). For safety-critical applications, use adversarial training or input validation.
Large language models including BERT can memorise and reproduce rare training examples. Audit fine-tuning data for PII before training on sensitive corpora.

BERT inherits biases from its Wikipedia and BookCorpus pretraining data. Gender, racial, and cultural biases in BERT embeddings are well-documented. Use debiasing techniques or bias-auditing tools before deploying in sensitive domains.
Always verify model file hashes when downloading from the Hub. A tampered model could execute malicious code during
BERT silently truncates inputs longer than max_position_embeddings (usually 512). Add an explicit check and log a warning when truncation occurs, so you know what content was lost.
BERT produces contextual token embeddings: the same word gets a different vector in different sentences, capturing polysemy and syntactic role simultaneously.
Input representation = token embedding + segment embedding (A/B) + learned position embedding, summed at each position.
Different layers encode different information: lower layers capture syntax (POS, morphology), upper layers capture semantics and task-specific features.
The [CLS] token's final hidden state is the conventional sentence representation for classification; mean-pooling all token embeddings works better for similarity tasks.
Fine-tuning all layers end-to-end almost always outperforms using frozen BERT embeddings as features. Use learning rate 2e-5 to 5e-5 with a warmup schedule.
For NER (token classification), sub-word tokens must have their labels aligned carefully, with -100 assigned to continuation tokens so the loss ignores them.
Domain-specific BERT variants (BioBERT, FinBERT, CodeBERT) consistently outperform bert-base on domain tasks; start with the closest domain checkpoint.
For production deployment, distil to DistilBERT or export to ONNX/TensorRT for 2–10× faster inference with minimal quality loss.
